sections.title: What are auxiliary verbs? (1)
sections.content: **Auxiliary verbs** (helping verbs) are used with a main verb to form questions, negatives, emphasis, and compound tenses. They do not usually carry the main meaning on their own.

sections.title: What are auxiliary verbs? (2)
sections.content:
**Primary auxiliaries** in English are **be** (am, is, are, was, were, been), **have** (have, has, had), and **do** (do, does, did).

In English we **cannot** say *I not know* or *She likes not* – we need an auxiliary: *I **do** not know*, *She **does** not like*.

sections.title: Do, does, did
sections.content:
**Do / does / did** are used for questions and negatives in the **present simple** and **past simple**.

• Present: *Do you know?* *She **doesn't** like it.* (Third person: **does** / **doesn't**.)
• Past: *Did they come?* *I **didn't** see him.*

After **do**, **does**, or **did** we always use the **base form** of the main verb (go, know, like), not the past or -s form.

sections.title: Be and have
sections.content:
**Be** is used for continuous tenses (be + -ing): *I am working.* *She was leaving.*

**Be** is also used in passives (be + past participle): *The letter was sent.*

**Have** is used for perfect tenses (have + past participle): *I have finished.* *She had left.*

Modals (will, can, must, etc.) are a separate group – see the topic **Modal Verbs**.

sections.title: Common mistakes 1 (1)
sections.content: In many languages there is no auxiliary like **do** for most questions and negatives. In English we must add **do / does / did** and keep the main verb in the **base form**.

sections.title: Common mistakes 1 (2)
sections.content:
• **Omitting do/does/did:** Wrong: *I not understand.* *She not like it.* Correct: *I **do** not understand.* *She **does** not like it.*

• **Wrong form with he/she/it:** Wrong: *He don't know.* *She don't have.* Correct: *He **doesn't** know.* *She **doesn't** have.* (Third person singular → **does** / **doesn't**.)

sections.title: Common mistakes 2 (1)
sections.content:
• **Have as main verb** (possess): Wrong: *Have you a car?* Correct: *Do you have a car?* or *Have you got a car?* (BrE)

• **Auxiliary before subject in questions:** Wrong: *Why you did go?* Correct: *Why **did** you go?*

sections.title: Common mistakes 2 (2)
sections.content:
• **Base form after did:** Wrong: *Did he went?* *She doesn't knows.* Correct: *Did he **go**?* *She doesn't **know**.*

• **Present perfect vs past:** Wrong: *I have been in London yesterday.* Correct: *I **went** yesterday* (specific time) or *I **have been** to London* (experience, no time).

questions.question: Which auxiliary do we use for questions and negatives in the **present simple**?
questions.explanation: **Do** (I/you/we/they) and **does** (he/she/it) are used for questions and negatives in the present simple.

questions.question: Why is *She don't like it* wrong?

options.a: We must use 'doesn't' with he/she/it in present simple

questions.explanation: Third person singular (he/she/it) in present simple uses **does** / **doesn't**, not *do* / *don't*.

questions.question: After *did* in a question or negative, which form of the main verb do we use?
questions.explanation: After **did** we always use the **base form** of the verb: *Did he go?* not *Did he went?*

questions.question: Which is correct for *Have you a car?* (when 'have' means possess)?
questions.explanation: When **have** is a main verb (possess), we usually form questions with **Do you have...?** In British English *Have you got...?* is also common.

questions.question: Why is *I not know* wrong in English?

options.d: In present simple negative we need the auxiliary **do** + not

questions.explanation: English requires the auxiliary **do** in present simple negatives: *I **do** not know* / *I don't know*.

questions.question: Which is correct?
questions.explanation: After **did** we use the **base form**: go.

questions.question: Which is correct? (have = possess)
questions.explanation: For *have* as main verb (possess), we use **Do you have...?**

questions.question: Which is correct?
questions.explanation: Plural **they** → **don't**; after auxiliary, main verb is base form **have**.
